relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The people circle Seir until the season changes.
YHWH speaks movement after repetition.
The long circling must end.
Esau's descendants are treated as kin with a protected border.
Israel is forbidden to seize even a foothold of Seir.
Food and water must be purchased rather than taken.
Forty years of wilderness are remembered as provision without lack.
Israel passes its kin and turns toward Moab.
Moab's land is also placed beyond Israel's claim.
Ancient giant traditions remain embedded in the land's memory.
Names change across peoples while memory persists.
Seir itself bears the history of one people replacing another.
The people cross Zered.
Thirty-eight years close the generation of war.
The old camp passes away under severe judgment.
A generation ends before the next command comes.
YHWH speaks again.
Israel reaches Moab's border.
Ammon too receives a protected inheritance.
Its landscape also carries giant traditions.
Ammon remembers earlier peoples displaced before it.
Esau's settlement is recalled through the same pattern.
Caphtorite migration adds another layer of displacement.
The protected-border ethic gives way to war against Sihon.
Fear is projected outward as a weapon of conquest.
Yet the first message to Sihon is framed in peace.
Israel asks only for road passage.
Food and water will be purchased.
The request appeals to prior peaceful transit.
Sihon's refusal is interpreted through a hardened spirit.
The narrative turns refusal into Divine transfer of land.
Sihon comes to battle.
Israel defeats king, sons, and army.
The text then crosses into total destruction of men, women, and children.
Livestock and goods become spoil for the victors.
No fortified city remains beyond their reach.
Ammon's boundary still remains untouched.

contemplative_note: Deuteronomy 2 contains a moral fracture inside one chapter: Edom, Moab, and Ammon are respected through purchase and restraint, while Sihon's people are annihilated. The protected-border ethic can be carried forward; the genocide cannot.
